title: Compost activators and accelerators
subtitle: What actually works, what does not, and the free alternatives
excerpt: Bought compost activators contain nitrogen, microbes, or both. Most gardens already have better free alternatives. This is what the research supports and what you can use from your kitchen and garden.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic technical notes on compost activators. HDRA organic growing publications on composting amendments. Rothamsted Research work on nitrogen additions to compost systems.

glossaryTerms.definition: The ratio of carbon-rich (brown) material to nitrogen-rich (green) material in a compost heap. The optimal range for hot composting is 25:1 to 30:1 by weight; too much carbon slows decomposition, too much nitrogen causes anaerobic conditions and odour.

content.text: A compost heap decomposes slowly when the

is too high, the heap is too dry, or the microbial population is too sparse. An activator addresses one or more of these limitations. The most effective activator is free: a dense nitrogen source already in your kitchen or garden.

An activator adds nitrogen, microbes, or both. Nitrogen is the more reliably effective. Bought activators based on dried blood, bone meal, or urea are concentrated nitrogen sources. Activators based on microbial cultures are less reliable: any compost heap already has abundant microbes. Adding more rarely helps if nitrogen and moisture levels are adequate.
Fresh grass clippings: one of the highest readily-available nitrogen sources. Nitrogen content around 3-4 percent dry weight. Add a 100-150 mm layer after any brown addition.
Coffee grounds: nitrogen content around 2 percent dry weight. Add directly to the heap. Cafes sometimes give spent grounds free to collect.

Comfrey leaves or liquid: comfrey (Symphytum x uplandicum) accumulates nitrogen, potassium, and phosphorus from deep in the soil. A bucket of wilted comfrey leaves added to a slow heap provides both nitrogen and potassium. Comfrey liquid feed (wilted leaves steeped in water for two to three weeks and diluted at 1:15) can be used to water a dry heap.

Human urine: high in urea nitrogen (approximately 0.5 percent nitrogen by volume). Dilute 1:10 with water and apply to the centre of a slow heap. This is the oldest and most reliably documented compost activator in the horticultural literature.

Nettles: nitrogen content around 5-6 percent dry weight, higher than most other plant material. Cut and add young growth; or steep in water for one to two weeks to make a nettle feed comparable to comfrey liquid.

Adding finished compost (a few shovelfuls from the previous batch) to a new heap introduces a diverse microbial community and is the most reliably effective microbial activator. Any mature compost from a healthy heap contains billions of bacteria and fungi per gram. A handful spread through the new heap layers is more effective than a packet of commercial microbial powder.
When to use a bought activator

A bought activator is useful when the heap is entirely woody or carbon-rich and no other nitrogen source is available. Dried blood or blood fish and bone raises the nitrogen level in a woody heap quickly. Use it on a heap of shredded hedge clippings or woodchip that would otherwise take two or three years to break down.
Adding yeast: yeast ferments sugars but does not contribute to the cellulose and lignin decomposition that makes up most compost. No measurable effect on a normal heap.
Adding rock dust or minerals: these may improve finished compost quality but do not speed up decomposition.

Adding worms to a hot heap: composting worms (Eisenia fetida) are killed at temperatures above 35 degrees C. They should not be added to a hot heap; they are appropriate for a cool worm bin or a mature cold heap.
